instruction: Explain std::atomic memory orderings in C++. When do you need memory_order_acquire, memory_order_release, and memory_order_seq_cst?
output:
<think>
Memory orderings control how atomic operations synchronize memory visibility between threads. The default seq_cst (sequentially consistent) provides the strongest guarantee — all threads see atomic operations in the same total order. It's the safest but most expensive because it requires full memory barriers. Release-acquire is weaker but sufficient for most producer-consumer patterns: a store with release guarantees that all previous writes are visible to any thread that does an acquire load of the same atomic variable. This creates a happens-before relationship without the overhead of total ordering. Relaxed ordering provides no synchronization at all — it's just atomic (no torn reads/writes) but other threads might see operations in any order. Use relaxed for counters where you don't care about ordering relative to other data, release-acquire for flag-based synchronization and lock-free data structures, and seq_cst when you need all threads to agree on the order of operations (rare, usually at the cost of performance).
</think>

```cpp
#include <atomic>
#include <iostream>
#include <thread>
#include <vector>
#include <cassert>

// === RELAXED: Just atomic, no ordering guarantees ===
// Use for: counters, statistics — when you don't care about ordering

std::atomic<int> counter{0};

void increment_counter(int n) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        // Relaxed: no synchronization with other data
        // Just ensures no torn read/write of the counter itself
        counter.fetch_add(1, std::memory_order_relaxed);
    }
}

// === RELEASE-ACQUIRE: Producer-consumer synchronization ===
// Store-release pairs with load-acquire to create happens-before

struct Message {
    int data[3];
};

std::atomic<bool> ready{false};
Message shared_msg;

void producer() {
    // Write data BEFORE setting flag
    shared_msg.data[0] = 42;
    shared_msg.data[1] = 43;
    shared_msg.data[2] = 44;

    // RELEASE: all writes above are guaranteed visible
    // to any thread that acquires this atomic
    ready.store(true, std::memory_order_release);
}

void consumer() {
    // ACQUIRE: sees all writes that happened before the release
    while (!ready.load(std::memory_order_acquire)) {
        // Spin wait
    }
    // GUARANTEED: shared_msg is fully written
    assert(shared_msg.data[0] == 42);
    assert(shared_msg.data[1] == 43);
    assert(shared_msg.data[2] == 44);
}

// === SEQ_CST: Total ordering across all threads ===
// Most expensive but simplest to reason about

std::atomic<bool> x{false}, y{false};
std::atomic<int> z{0};

void write_x() { x.store(true, std::memory_order_seq_cst); }
void write_y() { y.store(true, std::memory_order_seq_cst); }

void read_x_then_y() {
    while (!x.load(std::memory_order_seq_cst)) {}
    if (y.load(std::memory_order_seq_cst)) {
        z.fetch_add(1, std::memory_order_relaxed);
    }
}

void read_y_then_x() {
    while (!y.load(std::memory_order_seq_cst)) {}
    if (x.load(std::memory_order_seq_cst)) {
        z.fetch_add(1, std::memory_order_relaxed);
    }
}
// With seq_cst: z is ALWAYS >= 1 (total order guarantees one thread
// sees both writes). With acquire-release, z could be 0!

// === PRACTICAL: Lock-free SPSC queue with release-acquire ===

template <typename T, size_t N>
class SPSCQueue {
public:
    bool push(const T& item) {
        size_t head = head_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
        size_t next = (head + 1) % N;
        if (next == tail_.load(std::memory_order_acquire)) {
            return false;  // Full
        }
        buffer_[head] = item;
        head_.store(next, std::memory_order_release);  // Publish
        return true;
    }

    bool pop(T& item) {
        size_t tail = tail_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
        if (tail == head_.load(std::memory_order_acquire)) {
            return false;  // Empty
        }
        item = buffer_[tail];
        tail_.store((tail + 1) % N, std::memory_order_release);
        return true;
    }

private:
    T buffer_[N];
    std::atomic<size_t> head_{0};
    std::atomic<size_t> tail_{0};
};

int main() {
    // Counter with relaxed ordering
    std::vector<std::thread> threads;
    for (int i = 0; i < 4; ++i)
        threads.emplace_back(increment_counter, 100000);
    for (auto& t : threads) t.join();
    std::cout << "Counter: " << counter.load() << std::endl; // 400000

    // Producer-consumer with release-acquire
    std::thread p(producer);
    std::thread c(consumer);
    p.join(); c.join();
    std::cout << "Message received safely\n";
}
```

instruction: How do you design a thread pool in C++? Show a practical implementation with task queuing and graceful shutdown.
output:
<think>
A thread pool pre-creates a fixed number of worker threads and distributes tasks to them via a shared queue. This avoids the overhead of creating/destroying threads per task. The key design decisions are: how to queue tasks (mutex + condition variable is standard), how to signal shutdown (poison pill vs atomic flag + notify_all), and how to handle return values (std::future via std::packaged_task). The worker threads loop: wait on condition variable, dequeue task, execute it. Graceful shutdown means: stop accepting new tasks, let existing tasks complete, then join threads. A common mistake is not notifying all workers during shutdown — some might be blocked on the condition variable forever. Another subtlety is exception safety — if a task throws, the worker thread should catch it and continue processing other tasks, not crash the whole pool. Using std::packaged_task with std::future provides a clean way to get results from async tasks.
</think>

```cpp
#include <condition_variable>
#include <functional>
#include <future>
#include <iostream>
#include <mutex>
#include <queue>
#include <thread>
#include <vector>

class ThreadPool {
public:
    explicit ThreadPool(size_t num_threads) : stop_(false) {
        for (size_t i = 0; i < num_threads; ++i) {
            workers_.emplace_back([this, i] { worker_loop(i); });
        }
    }

    // Submit task and get future for the result
    template <typename F, typename... Args>
    auto submit(F&& f, Args&&... args)
        -> std::future<std::invoke_result_t<F, Args...>> {

        using ReturnType = std::invoke_result_t<F, Args...>;

        auto task = std::make_shared<std::packaged_task<ReturnType()>>(
            std::bind(std::forward<F>(f), std::forward<Args>(args)...)
        );

        std::future<ReturnType> result = task->get_future();

        {
            std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(mutex_);
            if (stop_) {
                throw std::runtime_error("ThreadPool is stopped");
            }
            tasks_.emplace([task]() { (*task)(); });
        }

        cv_.notify_one();  // Wake one worker
        return result;
    }

    // Graceful shutdown: finish pending tasks, then stop
    ~ThreadPool() {
        {
            std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(mutex_);
            stop_ = true;
        }
        cv_.notify_all();  // Wake ALL workers so they can exit

        for (auto& worker : workers_) {
            if (worker.joinable()) {
                worker.join();
            }
        }
    }

    size_t pending() const {
        std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(mutex_);
        return tasks_.size();
    }

    // Non-copyable, non-movable
    ThreadPool(const ThreadPool&) = delete;
    ThreadPool& operator=(const ThreadPool&) = delete;

private:
    void worker_loop(size_t id) {
        while (true) {
            std::function<void()> task;
            {
                std::unique_lock<std::mutex> lock(mutex_);
                cv_.wait(lock, [this] {
                    return stop_ || !tasks_.empty();
                });

                if (stop_ && tasks_.empty()) {
                    return;  // Graceful exit
                }

                task = std::move(tasks_.front());
                tasks_.pop();
            }

            // Execute task outside the lock
            try {
                task();
            } catch (const std::exception& e) {
                // Task threw — log but don't kill the worker
                std::cerr << "Worker " << id << " task error: "
                          << e.what() << std::endl;
            }
        }
    }

    std::vector<std::thread> workers_;
    std::queue<std::function<void()>> tasks_;
    mutable std::mutex mutex_;
    std::condition_variable cv_;
    bool stop_;
};

int main() {
    ThreadPool pool(4);

    // Submit tasks and collect futures
    std::vector<std::future<int>> futures;
    for (int i = 0; i < 20; ++i) {
        futures.push_back(pool.submit([i]() -> int {
            std::this_thread::sleep_for(std::chrono::milliseconds(50));
            return i * i;
        }));
    }

    // Collect results
    for (auto& f : futures) {
        std::cout << f.get() << " ";
    }
    std::cout << std::endl;

    // Pool destructor joins all threads gracefully
    // Pending tasks complete, then workers exit
}
```

instruction: Explain condition variables in C++ and the common pitfalls: spurious wakeups and lost notifications.
output:
<think>
Condition variables are the standard mechanism for thread synchronization in C++ when one thread needs to wait for a condition that another thread will make true. The pattern is: lock a mutex, check a predicate, if false then wait (which atomically releases the mutex and sleeps), when notified re-acquire the mutex and re-check the predicate. Two critical pitfalls: spurious wakeups — the OS can wake a waiting thread without any notification, so you MUST check the predicate in a loop, not just once. The wait-with-predicate overload handles this automatically. Lost notifications — if notify is called before wait, the notification is lost (condition variables don't have memory). This happens when the producer signals before the consumer starts waiting. The fix is to always check the predicate before waiting — if the condition is already true, don't wait at all. Another subtle issue is calling notify while holding the mutex — this is technically correct but can cause the woken thread to immediately block on the mutex, wasting a context switch.
</think>

```cpp
#include <condition_variable>
#include <iostream>
#include <mutex>
#include <queue>
#include <thread>
#include <chrono>

// === CORRECT pattern: Bounded queue with condition variables ===

template <typename T>
class BoundedQueue {
public:
    explicit BoundedQueue(size_t capacity) : capacity_(capacity) {}

    void push(T item) {
        std::unique_lock<std::mutex> lock(mutex_);

        // Wait with predicate — handles spurious wakeups automatically
        // Equivalent to: while (queue_.size() >= capacity_) not_full_.wait(lock);
        not_full_.wait(lock, [this] {
            return queue_.size() < capacity_;
        });

        queue_.push(std::move(item));

        // Notify AFTER modifying state, can unlock first for efficiency
        lock.unlock();
        not_empty_.notify_one();
    }

    T pop() {
        std::unique_lock<std::mutex> lock(mutex_);

        not_empty_.wait(lock, [this] {
            return !queue_.empty();
        });

        T item = std::move(queue_.front());
        queue_.pop();

        lock.unlock();
        not_full_.notify_one();
        return item;
    }

    bool try_pop(T& item, std::chrono::milliseconds timeout) {
        std::unique_lock<std::mutex> lock(mutex_);

        // Timed wait — returns false on timeout
        if (!not_empty_.wait_for(lock, timeout, [this] {
            return !queue_.empty();
        })) {
            return false;  // Timed out
        }

        item = std::move(queue_.front());
        queue_.pop();
        lock.unlock();
        not_full_.notify_one();
        return true;
    }

private:
    std::queue<T> queue_;
    size_t capacity_;
    std::mutex mutex_;
    std::condition_variable not_empty_;  // Signaled when item added
    std::condition_variable not_full_;   // Signaled when item removed
};

// === PITFALL 1: Spurious wakeups ===

void bad_wait_example() {
    std::mutex m;
    std::condition_variable cv;
    bool ready = false;

    // ❌ BAD: Single if-check — spurious wakeup skips the wait
    // std::unique_lock<std::mutex> lock(m);
    // if (!ready) cv.wait(lock);  // Might return without notify!
    // assume_ready();  // BUG: might not actually be ready

    // ✅ GOOD: Loop check (or use predicate overload)
    std::unique_lock<std::mutex> lock(m);
    cv.wait(lock, [&ready] { return ready; });  // Re-checks after every wake
}

// === PITFALL 2: Lost notification ===

void lost_notification_demo() {
    std::mutex m;
    std::condition_variable cv;
    bool data_ready = false;

    // Producer runs FIRST
    std::thread producer([&] {
        {
            std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(m);
            data_ready = true;
        }
        cv.notify_one();  // Notification fired, but no one is waiting yet!
    });

    producer.join();  // Producer finishes

    // Consumer starts AFTER — notification already lost!
    // But with predicate check, this STILL works:
    std::unique_lock<std::mutex> lock(m);
    cv.wait(lock, [&data_ready] { return data_ready; });
    // ✅ Predicate is already true → wait returns immediately
    // The predicate check is what prevents lost notifications
}

int main() {
    BoundedQueue<int> queue(5);

    std::thread producer([&queue] {
        for (int i = 0; i < 20; ++i) {
            queue.push(i);
            std::cout << "Produced: " << i << std::endl;
        }
    });

    std::thread consumer([&queue] {
        for (int i = 0; i < 20; ++i) {
            int val = queue.pop();
            std::cout << "Consumed: " << val << std::endl;
        }
    });

    producer.join();
    consumer.join();
}
```

instruction: Show how to build a simple lock-free stack in C++ using std::atomic and compare-and-swap. Explain the ABA problem.
output:
<think>
Lock-free data structures use atomic compare-and-swap (CAS) operations instead of mutexes. The basic idea for a lock-free stack: to push, read the current head, set your new node's next to point to head, then CAS head from old to new. If another thread modified head between your read and CAS, the CAS fails and you retry. Pop is similar: read head, read head->next, CAS head from old to next. The ABA problem is the critical pitfall: thread 1 reads head=A, gets preempted. Thread 2 pops A, pops B, pushes A back. Thread 1 resumes, sees head is still A, CAS succeeds — but A->next is now different! The stack is corrupted. Solutions include: tagged pointers (incrementing a version counter alongside the pointer), hazard pointers, or epoch-based reclamation. In C++, you can use a struct with pointer + counter in a single atomic using compare_exchange. Memory reclamation is the hardest part of lock-free programming — you can't just delete a popped node because another thread might be reading it.
</think>

```cpp
#include <atomic>
#include <iostream>
#include <memory>
#include <thread>
#include <vector>

// Simple lock-free stack (educational — see ABA discussion below)
template <typename T>
class LockFreeStack {
    struct Node {
        T data;
        Node* next;
        Node(T val) : data(std::move(val)), next(nullptr) {}
    };

    std::atomic<Node*> head_{nullptr};

public:
    void push(T value) {
        Node* new_node = new Node(std::move(value));
        // CAS loop: keep trying until we successfully update head
        new_node->next = head_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
        while (!head_.compare_exchange_weak(
            new_node->next,  // Expected: current head
            new_node,        // Desired: our new node
            std::memory_order_release,
            std::memory_order_relaxed
        )) {
            // CAS failed — another thread changed head
            // new_node->next is updated to current head automatically
            // Retry with updated value
        }
    }

    bool pop(T& result) {
        Node* old_head = head_.load(std::memory_order_acquire);
        while (old_head != nullptr) {
            // ⚠️ DANGER: Reading old_head->next — old_head might be freed!
            // This is the ABA problem zone
            if (head_.compare_exchange_weak(
                old_head,          // Expected: current head
                old_head->next,    // Desired: second element
                std::memory_order_release,
                std::memory_order_relaxed
            )) {
                result = std::move(old_head->data);
                delete old_head;  // ⚠️ Only safe if no other thread holds this
                return true;
            }
            // CAS failed — old_head updated, retry
        }
        return false;  // Stack is empty
    }

    ~LockFreeStack() {
        T dummy;
        while (pop(dummy)) {}  // Drain remaining items
    }
};

// === ABA PROBLEM EXPLAINED ===
// Initial:    head → A → B → C
//
// Thread 1: reads head=A, about to CAS head from A to B
// Thread 1: PREEMPTED
//
// Thread 2: pops A → head=B
// Thread 2: pops B → head=C
// Thread 2: pushes A back → head=A→C (A is recycled!)
//
// Thread 1: resumes, sees head==A (same pointer!)
// Thread 1: CAS succeeds! head = A->next
// But A->next is now C, not B! B is lost!

// === FIX: Tagged pointer (version counter) ===
template <typename T>
class SafeLockFreeStack {
    struct Node {
        T data;
        Node* next;
        Node(T val) : data(std::move(val)), next(nullptr) {}
    };

    struct TaggedPtr {
        Node* ptr;
        size_t tag;  // Version counter — prevents ABA
    };

    std::atomic<TaggedPtr> head_{{nullptr, 0}};

public:
    void push(T value) {
        Node* new_node = new Node(std::move(value));
        TaggedPtr old_head = head_.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
        TaggedPtr new_head;
        do {
            new_node->next = old_head.ptr;
            new_head = {new_node, old_head.tag + 1};  // Increment tag
        } while (!head_.compare_exchange_weak(old_head, new_head,
                  std::memory_order_release, std::memory_order_relaxed));
    }

    bool pop(T& result) {
        TaggedPtr old_head = head_.load(std::memory_order_acquire);
        while (old_head.ptr != nullptr) {
            TaggedPtr new_head = {old_head.ptr->next, old_head.tag + 1};
            if (head_.compare_exchange_weak(old_head, new_head,
                std::memory_order_release, std::memory_order_relaxed)) {
                result = std::move(old_head.ptr->data);
                delete old_head.ptr;
                return true;
            }
        }
        return false;
    }
};

int main() {
    LockFreeStack<int> stack;
    constexpr int N = 10000;

    // Concurrent push from multiple threads
    std::vector<std::thread> threads;
    for (int t = 0; t < 4; ++t) {
        threads.emplace_back([&stack, t]() {
            for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
                stack.push(t * N + i);
            }
        });
    }
    for (auto& t : threads) t.join();

    int count = 0;
    int val;
    while (stack.pop(val)) count++;
    std::cout << "Popped " << count << " items (expected " << 4 * N << ")\n";
}
```
